title: Ensure 'Java version' is currently supported (if in use)

input_row.title: Ensure 'Java version' is currently supported (if in use)
input_row.description: Periodically, older versions of Java may be deprecated and no longer supported. Using a supported version of Java for function app deployment slots is recommended to avoid potential unpatched vulnerabilities.
input_row.rationale: Deprecated and unsupported versions of programming and scripting languages can present vulnerabilities which may not be addressed or may not be addressable.
input_row.audit: Take note of currently supported versions of Java here: https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/java-se-support-roadmap.html Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services or Function App. 2. Click the name of a function app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, ensure that for a Stack of Java, the Java Version reflects a currently supported release. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each function app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list function apps: az functionapp list For each function app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az functionapp deployment slot list --resource-group -- name For each deployment slot, run the following command to get the Java version: az resource show --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --query properties.[javaContainer,javaContainerVersion,javaVersion,linuxFxVersion] If Java is in use, ensure the version is currently supported. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: e1d1b522-02b0-4d18-a04f-5ab62d20445f - Name: 'Function app slots that use Java should use a specified 'Java version''
input_row.remediation: Note: No action is required if Java is not in use. Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services or Function App. 2. Click the name of a function app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, for a Stack of Java, set the Java Version to a currently supported release. 7. Click Save. 8. Click Continue. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each function app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI Run the following command to list supported runtimes: az functionapp list-runtimes For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command with the appropriate parameters to update the Java version: az resource update --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --set properties.[javaContainer|javaContainerVersion|javaVersion|linuxFxVersion]="< java-container|java-container-version|java-version|java-runtime-version>"
input_row.impact: If your app is written using version-dependent features or libraries, they may not be available on more recent versions. If you wish to update, research the impact thoroughly.
input_row.default_value: The version is selected during creation.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Navigate to the AWS Console:** Log in to the [AWS Management Console](https://aws.amazon.com/console/) and open the AWS Lambda console by searching for "Lambda" in the services search bar.

2. **Select the Function:** In the Lambda console, find the function you want to audit from the list. Click on the name of the function to open the function details.

3. **Check the Configuration:** From the function detail page, scroll down to the "Configuration" tab. Under "Runtime settings", you will find the "Runtime" listed.

4. **Verify the Java Version:** Look for the runtime version. If the function uses Java, check the specific version. AWS generally supports only a few versions; you can refer to the [AWS Lambda runtime support policy](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-runtimes.html) to confirm if the version is currently supported.

5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** The current version of Java should be listed as supported according to the AWS Lambda runtime support policy.

6. **Confirm Compliance:** If the runtime version is Java and it is one of the supported versions as per the AWS policy, the function is compliant. If it is an unsupported version, it is non-compliant and needs remediation.

**Note:** It is critical to ensure that if Java is used, it aligns with AWS’s support policy to mitigate potential vulnerabilities from deprecated versions.
